questions.question: Explain the cyclic property of cyclic codes with an example.
keyPoints: Definition: any cyclic shift of valid codeword is also valid codeword
If c = (c₀,c₁,...,cₙ₋₁) is codeword, then shift by i positions gives valid codeword
Example: if 1011 is codeword for n=4, then 1101, 1110, 0111 are all codewords
This property enables efficient hardware implementation using shift registers

questions.question: Derive the relationship between generator polynomial g(x) and parity check polynomial h(x) for a cyclic code.
keyPoints: For code length n, we have xⁿ - 1 = g(x) × h(x)
Parity check polynomial h(x) = (xⁿ - 1)/g(x)
If deg(g) = r, then deg(h) = n - r = k
This relationship is used for encoding and syndrome computation

questions.question: Describe the syndrome decoding method for cyclic codes.
keyPoints: Compute syndrome s(x) = r(x) mod h(x) where r(x) is received polynomial
If s(x) = 0, no error detected
Use syndrome table to look up error pattern e(x) corresponding to s(x)
Decode as c(x) = r(x) - e(x) = r(x) + e(x) in GF(2)
Method effective for short codes but complexity grows with 2^(n-k)

questions.question: Explain systematic encoding of cyclic codes with polynomial representation.
keyPoints: Given message polynomial m(x) of degree < k
Compute parity bits by finding remainder: p(x) = m(x) × xʳ mod g(x) where r = n-k
Codeword is c(x) = m(x) × xʳ + p(x)
Systematic form has message bits followed by parity bits
This ensures efficient error detection using parity check matrix

questions.question: What is the minimum distance of a cyclic code? How does it determine error-correcting capability?
keyPoints: Minimum distance d = minimum Hamming weight of non-zero codewords
Error-correcting capability t = ⌊(d-1)/2⌋
Error-detecting capability = d-1
Example: d=3 can correct t=1 error or detect up to 2 errors
Minimum distance derived from generator polynomial structure

questions.question: Explain how cyclic codes are implemented using Linear Feedback Shift Registers (LFSR).
keyPoints: LFSR implements division by generator polynomial g(x)
Contains m = degree of g(x) flip-flops for storage
Feedback connections correspond to polynomial coefficients
For encoding: message shifted through with feedback from g(x)
For syndrome: received bits shifted through register computing remainder

questions.question: Define primitive polynomial and its role in cyclic codes.
keyPoints: Primitive polynomial of degree m divides xⁿ - 1 only for n = 2ᵐ - 1
It generates maximal length sequences (m-sequences)
Used in construction of cyclic codes with good properties
If α is root of primitive polynomial in extension field GF(2ᵐ), then α has order 2ᵐ - 1
Important for BCH codes and Golay codes

questions.question: Construct the (7,4) cyclic Hamming code. Find generator polynomial, generator matrix, parity check matrix, and encode message 1011.
keyPoints: Generator polynomial g(x) = 1 + x + x³ (divides x⁷ - 1)
Degree of g(x) = 3, so n-k=3, k=4
Generator matrix rows: [g(x)], [x·g(x)], [x²·g(x)], [x³·g(x)] in polynomial form
Parity check h(x) = (x⁷-1)/g(x) = 1 + x + x² + x⁴
Encoding 1011: m(x)=1+x³+x²
Compute p(x)=m(x)·x³ mod g(x) = x³+x⁶ mod g(x) = 1+x

questions.question: Explain error-trapping decoder for cyclic codes with a diagram and example.
keyPoints: Error-trapping decodes burst errors efficiently
Steps: (1) Compute syndrome s(x) = r(x) mod h(x)
(2) If syndrome non-zero, cyclic shift received word
(3) After shifts, error pattern may get trapped in lower degree positions
(4) Correct trapped error, then reverse cyclic shifts
Example: For (7,4) code with error pattern in positions 2,5
Shift until error in positions 0,1 then correct
Reverse shift to get original positions
Efficient for burst length ≤ r where r = n-k

questions.question: Discuss the properties and construction of BCH codes as cyclic codes. Explain how designed distance determines error correction capability.
keyPoints: BCH (Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem) codes are cyclic
Designed distance δ determines minimum distance d ≥ δ
For t-error correcting BCH: d = 2t + 1
Generator polynomial has roots: α, α², ..., α^(2t) where α is primitive element
Construct g(x) = LCM of minimal polynomials of these roots
Length n = 2^m - 1 for binary BCH
Example: t=1, δ=3 gives Hamming code with g(x) having roots α, α²
Parity check bits = mt, so code dimension k = n - mt
BCH codes achieve excellent performance near Shannon limit

questions.question: Compare cyclic codes with general linear block codes. Discuss advantages of cyclic structure in hardware implementation.
keyPoints: Linear codes: any linear combination of codewords is codeword
Cyclic codes: additionally, cyclic shift of codeword is codeword
Advantages of cyclic structure:
(1) Efficient encoding using shift registers - simple circuit implementation
(2) Syndrome computation uses same division circuit
(3) Algebraic structure allows systematic decoding algorithms
(4) Error-trapping decoder exploits cyclic property
(5) Easy to analyze using polynomial mathematics
(6) Popular applications: CRC (error detection), CD players (Reed-Solomon)
Disadvantage: not all linear codes are cyclic, limited code rates
Examples: CRC-32, Hamming codes, Reed-Solomon are all cyclic
